title: Psalms 85
subtitle: Will You Not Revive Us Again?

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “Restore Us Again, God Our Saviour”

heb.gloss: revive us / give us life again
heb.paragraph: "Will you not revive us (təḥayyēnū) again, that your people may rejoice in you?" (v.6). The community recalls past restoration: God forgave sin, covered transgressions, set aside wrath (vv.1–3). Now they ask: do it again. Revive us AGAIN. The request assumes God has done this before and can repeat it.

notes.note: "You restored the fortunes of Jacob; you forgave the iniquity of your people." Past grace as argument for present grace.
"Will you not revive us again? Show us your unfailing love and grant us your salvation." Revival = life restored. The path: ḥesed → salvation → joy.

refs.note: The promise to restore fortunes and gather from exile echoes the psalm's restoration language.
Cyrus's decree ending exile demonstrates the restoration the psalm celebrates.
Times of refreshing from the Lord's presence develop the psalm's revival theology.

hist.context: Psalm 85 is a communal prayer blending past thanksgiving (vv.1-3), present lament (vv.4-7), and prophetic oracle (vv.8-13). The reference to restored fortunes and forgiven sin (vv.1-3) may recall the return from Babylonian exile. Yet present distress prompts the question 'Will you be angry with us forever?' (v.5) — restoration is incomplete.

heb.paragraph: "Love (ḥesed) and faithfulness meet together; righteousness and peace kiss each other" (v.10). The psalm’s most famous verse — four divine attributes personified and reunited: love + faithfulness, righteousness + peace. In the fall, these were torn apart (you could have justice without mercy, or mercy without justice). At the cross, they are reconciled: all four meet and kiss.

notes.note: "Love and faithfulness meet together; righteousness and peace kiss each other." The four attributes of God that seemed to conflict are harmonised. The cross is where this verse finds its ultimate fulfilment: justice satisfied AND mercy extended.

"Faithfulness springs forth from the earth, and righteousness looks down from heaven. The LORD will indeed give what is good." Earth and heaven meet: faithfulness rises, righteousness descends. The vertical reunion.
"Righteousness goes before him and prepares the way for his steps." Righteousness as God’s advance party. The way is prepared.

refs.note: The Word becoming flesh and dwelling among us embodies the psalm's meeting of love and faithfulness.
God's righteousness and justice meeting in Christ's sacrifice fulfills the psalm's vision.
Christ as our peace reconciling enemies echoes the psalm's shalom theology.

hist.context: The prophetic oracle (vv.10-13) describes cosmic reconciliation: love and faithfulness meet, righteousness and peace kiss. This personification of divine attributes greeting each other portrays salvation as reunion of qualities torn apart by sin. The vision of righteousness going before the LORD and preparing the way anticipates messianic fulfillment.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The four attributes of God that seemed to conflict are harmonised…

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in Will You Not Revive Us Again?.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVRestore us again, God our Savior, and put away your displeasure toward us.ESVRestore us again, O God of our salvation, and put away your indignation toward us!NIVYou forgave the iniquity of your people and covered all their sins.ESVYou forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered all their sin. Selah

rec.text: ""You restored the fortunes of Jacob; you forgave the iniquity of your people." Past grace as argument for present grace."

rows.label: Revive Us — 85:1–7
rows.text: "Will you not revive us AGAIN?" Past grace argues for present grace.

hebtext.gloss: revive us / give us life again
hebtext.note: "Will you not revive us (təḥayyēnū) again, that your people may rejoice in you?" (v.6). The community recalls past restoration: God forgave sin, covered transgressions, set aside wrath (vv.1–3). Now they ask: do it again. Revive us AGAIN. The request assumes God has done this before and can repeat it.

"Love (ḥesed) and faithfulness meet together; righteousness and peace kiss each other" (v.10). The psalm’s most famous verse — four divine attributes personified and reunited: love + faithfulness, righteousness + peace. In the fall, these were torn apart (you could have justice without mercy, or mercy without justice). At the cross, they are reconciled: all four meet and kiss.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: the four attributes reconciled. Prophecy: the cross as the place where love/faithfulness meet righteousness/peace. Holiness: God’s attributes united.
